69
COMM Ic5netJ /,)q’)
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
March26, 1997
Cary Davidson
Reed & Davidson
777 South Figueroa Street
Los Angeles, California 90017
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-97-103
Dear Mr. Davidson:
This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1
QUESTIONS
What is the status of the individuals appearing on the ballot for a Los Angeles City
Municipal Election? Specifically, are they “candidates” subject to the contribution limits of
Proposition 208? Is the answer to the first question consistent with advice concerning the status
of individuals appearing on the ballot in recall elections? If not, what is the distinction?
2 Is the
answer to the first question consistent with advice concerning the status of individuals appearing
on the ballot when a new city is being incorporated?
CONCLUSIONS
Individuals appearing on the ballot for the Los Angeles City Charter Reform Commission
measure are not subject to the contribution limitations of Proposition 208. These candidates have
the same status as candidates in a recall election; that is, they are candidates subject to the
disclosure requirements of the Act, but are not subject to contribution limitations.
I Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 You also asked whether the answer to question I is consistent with advice concerning the status of
individuals appearing on the ballot when a new city is being incorporated. It is unclear what advice you are
referencing and what relation this question has to your initial request for assistance. Therefore, we decline to
address this hypothetical question. (Regulation I 8329(c)(4).)
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No. 1-97-103
Page 2
FACTS
You represent a variety of interests seeking to support or oppose persons running for
positions on the charter reform commission in the April 8, 1997, Los Angeles City Municipal
Election. A charter reform initiative will appear on the April 8, 1997, ballot in the City of Los
Angeles. Voters will vote on whether or not to form the charter reform commission and then will
choose the fifteen charter reform commission members from candidates appearing on the same
ballot.
ANALYSIS
According to your facts, the City of Los Angeles has contribution limitations in effect,
but they only apply to “elective city office” as that term is defined under the Los Angeles City
Charter. The term “elective city office” does not include the charter reform commission and,
thus, the city contribution limits do not apply. You have inquired whether the contribution
limitations in the Act would apply instead.
The Act, as recently amended by Proposition 208, prohibits candidates from receiving
contributions in excess of specific limits. (Section 85301.)
A candidate is defined, inter alia, as
“an individual who is listed on the ballot.” (Section 82007.) Arguably, since candidates for the
charter reform commission appear on the ballot, they could be considered candidates under the
Act and thus subject to the contribution limitations in Section 85301.
However, Section 82043 defines a “measure” as:
“[A]ny constitutional amendment or other proposition which is
submitted to a popular vote at an election by action of a legislative
body, or which is submitted or is intended to be submitted to a
popular vote at an election by initiative, referendum or recall
procedure whether or not it qualifies for the ballot.” (Section
82043.)
The United States Supreme Court has ruled that contribution limitations may not be applied to
ballot measure campaigns. (Citizens Against Rent Control v. City ofBerkeley (1981) 454 U.S.
290.) Proposition 208 has followed that decision and applied contribution limitations only to
candidate elections, not ballot measures. (See Section 85301.)
The charter reform proposal is a proposition submitted to a popular vote on April 8, 1997.
The charter reform initiative itself is a ballot measure as defmed by the Act. The individuals
seeking election to the charter commission are running as a part of the ballot measure. If the
ballot measure fails, they will not be elected to office. This is similar to a recall. A recall is
considered to be a ballot measure under the Act and candidates running in a recall election are
not subject to contribution limitations. (Cohen Advice Letter, 1-96-364; Roberti Advice Letter,
No. A-89-358.) Therefore, we conclude that the charter commission candidates are not subject to


File No. 1-97-103
Page 3
the contribution limitations set forth in Section 85301.
Please keep in mind that the individuals running for the charter reform commission are
not considered candidates for the purpose of contribution limitations, but are still considered
candidates for all other purposes. Accordingly, they are subject to all of the disclosure
requirements in Chapter 4 ofthe Act.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
E
By: Liane Randolph
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak
With the advent of Proposition 208’s contribution limits, the Commission may need to revisit the issue of
whether replacement candidate running for office as part of a recall attempt should be subject to the applicable
limits.

on the ballot when a new city is being incorporated?
